input.reflection:
Walked into the session with 9 open positions — well above my self-imposed 5-position cap. Either the cap was breached on prior days I'm not seeing, or positions were re-opened after my last memory of closes. Either way, today was a risk-management session, not an entry session.

Two clear actions: (1) APH reports earnings today — direct violation of my 7-day rule. Closed for -1.93%. (2) TER price gapped from $380 to $306 in 24 hours (-19%), pushing P&L to -5.51% and putting the stock 27.43% off its 52w high. This is exactly what evidence-rule 744aa229 ('far_from_high', win rate 43% vs 54%) warns against. Closed before stop trigger to preserve capital.

No new entries despite strong regime — the position book is already maxed and macro is mixed (VIX +5.5%, IWM weak). Trying to stuff more into a defensive tape would be activity for activity's sake.

Pattern observation worth encoding: when a 'super-tier' stock has earnings the same day, the scan is stale by hours. The earnings calendar must be checked against EVERY existing position daily, not just at entry. Today APH was held into earnings — that's a process gap. Going to add this as a rule: daily earnings sweep on open positions, not just on candidates.

Lesson from yesterday's KLAC observation (cushion is depreciating asset) is reinforced: APH had no cushion, TER had no cushion, both required exits driven by external events. The thesis "I'll exit when cushion thins" only works if I'm watching every day with discipline; cleaner is "exit T-1 to earnings unless thesis is compelling."

input.rule: Run earnings_on_deck() against ALL open positions at the start of every session, not just candidates. Any position reporting within 24-48 hours must be exited unless there is a substantial profit cushion (>10%) AND a clear bullish thesis for the print. The 7-day rule applies at entry; at exit, the standard tightens to 1-2 days.
input.reason: Today APH was carried into its earnings date because I checked earnings only on new candidates, not on existing holdings. This is a process gap, not bad luck — it will repeat unless codified. Yesterday's KLAC and today's APH both came uncomfortably close to binary risk because I wasn't sweeping the book daily.
